unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you will learn how to count in tens all the way to one hundred. You will name shapes like the circle, square, triangle and rectangle, and you will spot patterns that repeat. Best of all, you will learn to measure and compare things: which is bigger, which is longer, which is taller, and which is heavier. Measuring helps us understand the world and be fair to everyone. By the end, you will be ready to measure carefully and share fairly, just like Samira and her friends do in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher audiovisual lecture and learn a few vocabulary words each day.
Complete all six grammar practices, moving from guided examples to independent use.
Listen to the Unit readings, including A Fair Way to Measure, and answer the comprehension questions.
Complete all six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question quiz and student self-assessment.

visual.alt: Children measuring shapes and comparing classroom objects

readings.title: Shapes All Around Us
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Shapes are all around us. The sun is a circle. Many windows are squares or rectangles. Some roofs are triangles. A clock face is a circle. A book is a rectangle. When we know shapes, we can describe the things we see. We can also make patterns with shapes, like: circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle. What shapes do you see around you?

readings.title: A Counting Song
readings.passageScript:
Read and sing this song. Count in tens as you go:
10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers. / 40 little, 50 little, 60 little fingers. / 70 little, 80 little, 90 little fingers. / 100 little fingers in the air!

readings.title: Using Numbers in Class
readings.passageScript:
Listen to this story about children using numbers. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“The children in Class 2 are using numbers today. Selma is counting shapes on a chart. She counts 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 shapes altogether. Ishmael is measuring how far he can jump. He jumped 45 centimetres! Samira is looking at the clock. It is 2:30. Numbers help us count, measure and tell time.”

readings.title: How People Measured Long Ago
readings.passageScript:
Long ago, people did not have rulers. Listen and think:
“Long ago in Egypt, people measured with their fingers, hands and arms. Long ago in Rome, people measured in footsteps. Today we use a ruler and a metre stick, because they are the same for everyone.”

readings.title: A Fair Way to Measure
readings.theme: Measuring and comparing things — long, short, tall, big, heavy — and doing it fairly.
readings.setting: A small school near the market in a Somali town.
readings.passageScript:
In the town of Beledweyne, near the busy market, there was a small school with a shady tree in the yard. One morning, Teacher Nadia had good news.
“Children, we are going to make a new reading corner,” she said. “We need a mat that is long enough for six friends to sit on. Who can measure the space for me?”
Samira jumped up. “I can measure with my feet!” she said. She walked heel to toe across the floor. “One, two, three … the space is fourteen feet long!”
Isaac laughed. “Let me try too.” He walked across the same floor. “One, two, three … I count only eleven feet. You are wrong, Samira!”
“No, you are wrong!” said Samira. The two friends began to argue.
Selma looked down at their feet. “Wait,” she said kindly. “Look. Ishmael's feet are big. Samira's feet are small. That is why your answers are different. Feet are not a fair way to measure.”
Samira stopped. “You are right, Selma,” she said quietly. “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.”
Teacher Nadia smiled. “Well done, Selma. Long ago, people measured with their hands and feet. But hands and feet are all different sizes. So today we use a ruler and a metre stick. A metre is always the same length.”
She gave them a long metre stick. “There are one hundred centimetres in one metre,” she said. “Now measure carefully, and be patient.”
The children measured the floor together, slowly and carefully. It was three metres long. Samira wrote the number down. This time, nobody argued.
While they worked, Adam carried two baskets of cushions. “This basket is heavy,” he said, lifting the big one. “And this one is light.” Samira ran to help him carry the heavy basket, so the load was fair for both of them.
Selma held up two ribbons to tie the cushions. “This red ribbon is longer,” she said. “This green ribbon is shorter. We need the longer one for the big cushion.”
Soon the mat was ready. It looked wonderful. Everyone wanted to sit down first.
“I am the tallest, so I should sit first!” said Adam, standing up straight.
“That is not fair,” said Samira gently. “Being tall does not mean you go first. Let us all sit together.”
Adam thought for a moment. “You are right,” he said. “I am sorry.” He made room, and everyone sat down side by side.
Teacher Nadia clapped her hands. “Today you learned three important things,” she said. “You learned to measure carefully. You learned to be patient. And you learned to be fair. A fair measure is the same for everyone — and so is a kind heart.”
Samira looked at Ishmael and smiled. “Your feet are bigger than mine,” she said, “but our mat is the same for both of us.”
Isaac laughed. “And it is long enough for all six of us!”
The friends sat on their new mat under the shady tree and opened their books. Outside, the market was noisy and busy. But in the little reading corner, everything felt just right.

comprehension.question: What shape is the sun? What shape is a book?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: The sun is a circle. A book is a rectangle.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: The sun is a circle. A book is a rectangle.

comprehension.question: What pattern of shapes is in the text?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: We can also make patterns with shapes, like: circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: We can also make patterns with shapes, like: circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle.

comprehension.question: Look around your own room. Which shapes can you see? Make a list.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.

comprehension.question: What is being counted in the song?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Count in tens as you go: 10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Count in tens as you go: 10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers.

comprehension.question: How many fingers are in the air at the end?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: / 100 little fingers in the air!
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: / 100 little fingers in the air!

comprehension.question: How many children would need to raise their hands to show 100 fingers? (Hint: each child has 10 fingers.)
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Count in tens as you go: 10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers. / 40 little, 50 little, 60 little fingers. / 70 little, 80 little, 90 little fingers. / 100 little fingers in the air!
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Count in tens as you go: 10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers. / 40 little, 50 little, 60 little fingers. / 70 little, 80 little, 90 little fingers. / 100 little fingers in the air!

comprehension.question: How is Selma using numbers? How many shapes did she count?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Selma is counting shapes on a chart, and she counts 50 shapes altogether.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

comprehension.question: How far did Ishmael jump?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Ishmael is measuring how far he can jump.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Ishmael is measuring how far he can jump.

comprehension.question: What are three ways we use numbers in the story?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “The children in Class 2 are using numbers today.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “The children in Class 2 are using numbers today.

comprehension.question: What did people use to measure long ago?
comprehension.correctAnswer: People used body measures such as their fingers, hands, arms and feet.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

comprehension.question: Why is it better to use a ruler than to use our feet? (Think about big feet and small feet!)
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Today we use a ruler and a metre stick, because they are the same for everyone.”
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Today we use a ruler and a metre stick, because they are the same for everyone.”

comprehension.question: What did the children want to make?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Children, we are going to make a new reading corner,” she said.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Children, we are going to make a new reading corner,” she said.

comprehension.question: Why did Samira and Ishmael get different answers when they measured with their feet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Feet are not a fair way to measure.” Samira stopped.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Feet are not a fair way to measure.” Samira stopped.

comprehension.question: What did Teacher Nadia say they should use to measure instead? How many centimetres are in one metre?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.” Teacher Nadia smiled. One morning, Teacher Nadia had good news.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.” Teacher Nadia smiled. One morning, Teacher Nadia had good news.

comprehension.question: Which basket was heavy, and who helped carry it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “And this one is light.” Samira ran to help him carry the heavy basket, so the load was fair for both of them.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “And this one is light.” Samira ran to help him carry the heavy basket, so the load was fair for both of them.

comprehension.question: Why did Samira say it was not fair for Adam to sit first? What did Adam do then?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “I am the tallest, so I should sit first!” said Adam, standing up straight. “That is not fair,” said Samira gently.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “I am the tallest, so I should sit first!” said Adam, standing up straight. “That is not fair,” said Samira gently.

comprehension.question: Why are feet and hands not a fair way to measure? Why is a metre stick better?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Feet are not a fair way to measure.” Samira stopped. So today we use a ruler and a metre stick.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Feet are not a fair way to measure.” Samira stopped. So today we use a ruler and a metre stick.

comprehension.question: Teacher Nadia said, “A fair measure is the same for everyone.” How can you be fair to your friends?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.” Teacher Nadia smiled.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.” Teacher Nadia smiled.

comprehension.question: When have you had to be patient, like the children measuring carefully?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Now measure carefully, and be patient.” The children measured the floor together, slowly and carefully.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Now measure carefully, and be patient.” The children measured the floor together, slowly and carefully.

grammar.title: Comparing Two Things (–er than)
grammar.explanation: When we measure, we often compare two things. We want to say which one is bigger, longer, taller or heavier. To compare two things, we add -er to the describing word, and we use the word than.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Take a short describing word and add -er, then add than:
big → bigger:  “An elephant is bigger than a goat.”
long → longer:  “This ribbon is longer than that ribbon.”
tall → taller:  “Adam is taller than Ishmael.”
small → smaller:  “A mouse is smaller than a cat.”
Two words change their spelling a little:
big → bigger (double the g).   ·   heavy → heavier (the y becomes i).
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “This line is more long than that line.”  ✗
Fix: For short words we add -er, not “more”: “This line is longer than that line.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think “-er than” together, like one little song: bigg-er than, long-er than, tall-er than.
grammar.practice:
Add -er to the word and finish the sentence:
(long)  A snake is ______ than a worm.
(tall)  A tree is ______ than a bush.
(big)  An elephant is ______ than a mouse.
(small)  A stone is ______ than a rock.
(heavy)  A big basket is ______ than a small basket.

grammar.title: Comparing Two Things (–er than): Use It
grammar.explanation: When we measure, we often compare two things. We want to say which one is bigger, longer, taller or heavier. To compare two things, we add -er to the describing word, and we use the word than. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 5 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Take a short describing word and add -er, then add than:
big → bigger:  “An elephant is bigger than a goat.”
long → longer:  “This ribbon is longer than that ribbon.”
tall → taller:  “Adam is taller than Ishmael.”
small → smaller:  “A mouse is smaller than a cat.”
Two words change their spelling a little:
big → bigger (double the g).   ·   heavy → heavier (the y becomes i).
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “This line is more long than that line.”  ✗
Fix: For short words we add -er, not “more”: “This line is longer than that line.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think “-er than” together, like one little song: bigg-er than, long-er than, tall-er than.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: How Many?  There is / There are
grammar.explanation: When we count, we ask “How many…?” to find out the number of things. To answer, we say “There is…” for one thing and “There are…” for two or more things.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
One thing → There is:  “There is one sun in the sky.”
Two or more → There are:  “There are ten circles on the chart.”
To ask → How many … are there?:  “How many children are there?”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “There is ten circles.”  ✗    Fix: Ten is more than one, so we say “There are ten circles.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: One = is.  More than one = are.  If you can count more than one, use are.
grammar.practice:
Write is or are:
There ______ twenty children in the class.
There ______ one clock on the wall.
How many shapes ______ there?
There ______ fifty beans in the jar.
There ______ one teacher in the room.

grammar.title: How Many?  There is / There are: Use It
grammar.explanation: When we count, we ask “How many…?” to find out the number of things. To answer, we say “There is…” for one thing and “There are…” for two or more things. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 5 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
One thing → There is:  “There is one sun in the sky.”
Two or more → There are:  “There are ten circles on the chart.”
To ask → How many … are there?:  “How many children are there?”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “There is ten circles.”  ✗    Fix: Ten is more than one, so we say “There are ten circles.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: One = is.  More than one = are.  If you can count more than one, use are.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Words That Sound the Same
grammar.explanation: Some words sound exactly the same but mean different things and are spelled differently. In this unit about numbers, four of them are important. We must choose the right one when we write.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
one (the number 1)  and  won (to win a game):  “I won one race.”
two (the number 2)  and  too (also / as well):  “I have two books, and you have two, too.”
four (the number 4)  and  for (given to):  “These four mangoes are for you.”
eight (the number 8)  and  ate (past of eat):  “I ate breakfast at eight o'clock.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “I have to apples.”  ✗    Fix: The number is two: “I have two apples.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: The number words look like their friends: t-wo is the number, f-our is the number, eigh-t is the number. Read the whole sentence to choose the right word.
grammar.practice:
Choose the correct word:
I have ______ (two / too) hands.
She ______ (eight / ate) all her rice.
These sweets are ______ (four / for) my friends.
My team ______ (one / won) the game!
You can come ______ (two / too).

grammar.title: Words That Sound the Same: Use It
grammar.explanation: Some words sound exactly the same but mean different things and are spelled differently. In this unit about numbers, four of them are important. We must choose the right one when we write. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 5 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
one (the number 1)  and  won (to win a game):  “I won one race.”
two (the number 2)  and  too (also / as well):  “I have two books, and you have two, too.”
four (the number 4)  and  for (given to):  “These four mangoes are for you.”
eight (the number 8)  and  ate (past of eat):  “I ate breakfast at eight o'clock.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “I have to apples.”  ✗    Fix: The number is two: “I have two apples.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: The number words look like their friends: t-wo is the number, f-our is the number, eigh-t is the number. Read the whole sentence to choose the right word.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Count in Tens
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Find small objects (beans, stones or bottle caps). Put them in groups of ten. Point to each group and say:
“Ten … twenty … thirty … forty … fifty … sixty … seventy … eighty … ninety … one hundred!”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say each number clearly? Did you count in the right order?

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Name and Describe Shapes
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Draw or find a circle, square, triangle, rectangle and heart. For each one, say:
“This is a ______.”
“A ______ has ______ sides.”
“I can see a ______ in the ______.”

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Bigger, Longer, Taller
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play this comparing game with your AI tutor or your family:
Choose two things you can see, for example two pencils.
Say a comparing sentence: “This pencil is longer than that pencil.”
Try other words: “bigger than”, “taller than”, “heavier than”. Then swap turns.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Measurement Detective
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six objects and compare their length, height, size or weight aloud.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use a complete comparison such as The book is heavier than the pencil.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Shape and Pattern Talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Describe five shapes and explain a repeating pattern for a partner to continue.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Name shape features and say which part repeats.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - How Many Survey
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Ask six How many questions, count carefully, and answer with There is or There are.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Check that singular and plural forms agree.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 1 — Numbers in Words
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Finish these sentences, then write two more of your own:
There are ______ (number) children in my class.
I can count to ______ (number).
My house has ______ (number) windows.
writing.modelText: There are twenty pencils in the box. I counted forty pages in my book. Our class collected sixty bottle tops.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Describe and Compare
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Choose two objects in your home. Write two comparing sentences:
The ______ is bigger than the ______.
The ______ is longer than the ______.
Then draw the two objects and label which one is bigger.
writing.modelText: The red book is longer than the blue book. The box is heavier than the pencil. I used a ruler to measure carefully.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Story response: A Fair Way to Measure
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Measure two things at home (or compare them by looking). Write two sentences with -er than, for example: “The spoon is longer than the fork.”
Draw the new reading corner from the story. Write one sentence about who is tall, who is short, and what is big or small in your picture.
writing.modelText: The children disagreed about the size of the mat. They used a fair way to measure it. The mat was long enough for everyone to share.
writing.sentenceStarter: The children disagreed about the size of the mat.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 4 - My Measurement Table
writing.promptAndInstructions: Measure five objects and write one complete comparison sentence for each result.
writing.modelText: The desk is longer than the book. The water bottle is taller than the cup.
writing.sentenceStarter: The desk is longer than the book
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 5 - Explain a Pattern
writing.promptAndInstructions: Create a repeating shape pattern and explain the rule in four sentences.
writing.modelText: My pattern is circle, square, circle, square. Two shapes repeat in the same order.
writing.sentenceStarter: My pattern is circle, square, circle, square
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 6 - A Fair Way to Share
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six sentences explaining how careful counting or measuring can make sharing fair.
writing.modelText: We measured the pieces before sharing them. Each child received the same amount, so the result was fair.
writing.sentenceStarter: We measured the pieces before sharing them
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

activities.title: Activity 1 — Fill in the Blanks (Counting in Tens)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the missing numbers on the lines.
Ten, ______, thirty, ______, fifty.
Sixty, ______, eighty, ______, one hundred.
There are ______ tens in one hundred.

activities.title: Activity 2 — True or False (Shape Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A circle has four sides.
A square has four equal sides.
A triangle has three corners.
activities.answerSummary: 1. False (a circle has no sides)   2. True   3. True

activities.title: Activity 3 — Match the Shape to the Object
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Draw a line from each shape to an object that has that shape.
circle  →  ______        (the sun / a door / a slice of watermelon)
square  →  ______
triangle  →  ______
rectangle  →  ______
activities.answerSummary: circle → the sun   ·   square → a window   ·   triangle → a slice of watermelon   ·   rectangle → a door

activities.title: Activity 4 — Compare and Write
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Look at each pair. Write a comparing sentence with -er than.
an elephant and a mouse (big) → ______________________
a snake and a worm (long) → ______________________
a big stone and a small stone (heavy) → ______________________
activities.answerSummary: 1. An elephant is bigger than a mouse.   2. A snake is longer than a worm.   3. A big stone is heavier than a small stone.

activities.title: Activity 5 - Estimate, Measure, Check
activities.instructionsAndItems: Estimate and then measure six objects in centimetres. Compare each estimate with the result.
activities.answerSummary: Answers vary. Require complete, relevant work that follows every instruction and uses the target Unit language accurately.
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

activities.title: Activity 6 - Shape Hunt
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find two circles, two rectangles and two other shapes. Record each object and its shape.
activities.answerSummary: Answers vary. Require complete, relevant work that follows every instruction and uses the target Unit language accurately.
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

assignments.title: Let's Measure learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Complete 'Writing 1 — Numbers in Words' and record 'Speaking 1 — Count in Tens'. Submit both pieces after checking the writing and speaking rubrics.

quizzes.question: What does 'ten' mean?
quizzes.options: The number 10. | The number 20. | The number 30. | The number 40.
quizzes.explanation: There are ten fingers on my two hands.

quizzes.question: Which word means: The number 20.
quizzes.explanation: Twenty children are playing in the field.

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'thirty'?
quizzes.explanation: There are thirty days in the month of September.

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: Fifty people came to the celebration.

quizzes.question: Which word means: The number 60.
quizzes.explanation: sixty: The number 60. There are sixty minutes in one hour.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which word means: The number 70.
quizzes.explanation: seventy: The number 70. My grandfather is seventy years old.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'eighty'?
quizzes.explanation: eighty: The number 80. The tall tree is eighty feet high.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: She scored ____ points in the game.
quizzes.explanation: ninety: The number 90. She scored ninety points in the game.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.options: one hundred | ne hundred | one hundrede | one hundredd
quizzes.explanation: one hundred: The number 100. I can count to one hundred by tens!

teacherNotes.note:
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the text with me and explain the hard words?” “Can you play a counting-in-tens listening game with me?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Am I pronouncing the numbers correctly?” “What is the difference between ‘fifteen’ and ‘fifty’?” “What is the difference between ‘long’ and ‘tall’?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check if I spelled these number words correctly?” “Can you check if my comparing sentences are correct?”
Story: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?” “Can you check my comparing sentences?” “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?”
Grammar: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check my grammar answers?” “Why do we say ‘longer’ but ‘more colourful’?” “Can you give me five more sentences to practise ‘is’ and ‘are’?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me practise counting in tens?” “How do I write these numbers in numerals and in words?” “What is a fun way to remember counting in tens?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “What is the difference between a square and a rectangle?” “Can you show me patterns in everyday life?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “How do I talk about measurements in English?” “When do I use centimetres and when do I use metres?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “How do I compare sizes in English?” “What is the difference between ‘long’ and ‘tall’?”

Term placement approved. Outcome mappings, comprehension guidance, vocabulary enrichment, assessments, live sessions, rubrics, writing specifications and answer-key coverage completed in the approved reference model.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. twenty, forty   2. seventy, ninety   3. ten (10)
1. False (a circle has no sides)   2. True   3. True
circle → the sun   ·   square → a window   ·   triangle → a slice of watermelon   ·   rectangle → a door
1. An elephant is bigger than a mouse.   2. A snake is longer than a worm.   3. A big stone is heavier than a small stone.
1. longer   2. taller   3. bigger   4. smaller   5. heavier
1. are   2. is   3. are   4. are   5. is
1. two   2. ate   3. for   4. won   5. too

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: The sun is a circle. A book is a rectangle.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: The sun is a circle. A book is a rectangle.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: We can also make patterns with shapes, like: circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: We can also make patterns with shapes, like: circle, square, triangle, circle, square, triangle.

Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
Marking guidance: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Count in tens as you go: 10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Count in tens as you go: 10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: / 100 little fingers in the air!
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: / 100 little fingers in the air!

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Count in tens as you go: 10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers. / 40 little, 50 little, 60 little fingers. / 70 little, 80 little, 90 little fingers. / 100 little fingers in the air!
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Count in tens as you go: 10 little, 20 little, 30 little fingers. / 40 little, 50 little, 60 little fingers. / 70 little, 80 little, 90 little fingers. / 100 little fingers in the air!

Selma is counting shapes on a chart, and she counts 50 shapes altogether.
Marking guidance: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Ishmael is measuring how far he can jump.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Ishmael is measuring how far he can jump.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “The children in Class 2 are using numbers today.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “The children in Class 2 are using numbers today.

People used body measures such as their fingers, hands, arms and feet.
Marking guidance: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Today we use a ruler and a metre stick, because they are the same for everyone.”
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Today we use a ruler and a metre stick, because they are the same for everyone.”

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Children, we are going to make a new reading corner,” she said.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Children, we are going to make a new reading corner,” she said.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Feet are not a fair way to measure.” Samira stopped.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Feet are not a fair way to measure.” Samira stopped.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.” Teacher Nadia smiled. One morning, Teacher Nadia had good news.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.” Teacher Nadia smiled. One morning, Teacher Nadia had good news.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “And this one is light.” Samira ran to help him carry the heavy basket, so the load was fair for both of them.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “And this one is light.” Samira ran to help him carry the heavy basket, so the load was fair for both of them.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “I am the tallest, so I should sit first!” said Adam, standing up straight. “That is not fair,” said Samira gently.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “I am the tallest, so I should sit first!” said Adam, standing up straight. “That is not fair,” said Samira gently.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Feet are not a fair way to measure.” Samira stopped. So today we use a ruler and a metre stick.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Feet are not a fair way to measure.” Samira stopped. So today we use a ruler and a metre stick.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.” Teacher Nadia smiled.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “We need one measure that is the same for everyone.” Teacher Nadia smiled.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Now measure carefully, and be patient.” The children measured the floor together, slowly and carefully.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Now measure carefully, and be patient.” The children measured the floor together, slowly and carefully.
Correct answer: The number 10.. There are ten fingers on my two hands.
Correct answer: twenty. Twenty children are playing in the field.
Correct answer: Number word. There are thirty days in the month of September.
Correct answer: forty. The farmer has forty goats.
Correct answer: fifty. Fifty people came to the celebration.
